<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after UP
prev_seg = previous_frame.segmentation
curr_seg = current_frame.segmentation

prev_nodes = prev_seg['nodes']
curr_nodes = curr_seg['nodes']

print("Before UP: %d nodes" % len(prev_nodes))
print("After UP: %d nodes" % len(curr_nodes))

for i, (pn, cn) in enumerate(zip(prev_nodes, curr_nodes)):
    if pn['boundary'] != cn['boundary']:
        print("Node %d moved: %s -> %s" % (i, pn['boundary'], cn['boundary']))

# Check if new nodes
prev_ids = set(n['id'] for n in prev_nodes]
curr_ids = set(n['id'] for n in curr_nodes]
print("New nodes: %s" % (curr_ids - prev_ids)
print("Removed nodes: %s" % (prev_ids - curr_ids)

# Check last action result
print("Last action result: %s" % last_action_result)

sections.content:
Now I can see what changed after UP:
- Node 9 (white square) moved from row 48 to row 44 (up by 4)
- Node 10 (green rectangle) moved from row 49-51 to 45-47 (up by 4)
- Node 11 (pink bar) shrunk from 63,63 to 63,62 (shrunk by 1)
- Node count went from 12 to 13 nodes
So UP moves the white square (node 9 and green rectangle (node 10) UP by 4 pixels. The pink bar shrunk. This looks like a movement game where the player is the white square and green objects move together. The pink bar is a timer/progress bar.
Let me try more UP to see if they're moving up. The white square (node 9) is the player. The green rectangle (node 10) seems to be the player character, and the green rectangle (node 10) is probably the player's body or a platform.
Let me try moving UP more to see if they reach a target. Let me also check what the blue framed objects are - they might be targets or obstacles.
Let me try pressing UP again to see if they move further up.
